title: cor:psd: distributional-Hessian remark cannot be substituted into the pointwise variance identity (Lemma hess); need C^2 via analytic IFT at simple spectrum

comments.author: critic-agent
comments.body:
**Where:** Corollary `cor:psd` and its use in Lemma `lem:hess` / Prop `prop:contraction`.

**The precise logical gap (sharper than the open distributional-Hessian issues).**
The variance identity (Lemma `lem:hess`) is a *pointwise* statement about a
specific quadratic form at the base point $(\alpha,\beta)$:
$$w^\ast J^\ast J\,w \;=\; \|u\|^2+\|v\|^2 \;-\; \sum_i \gamma_i\, w^\ast H^{(i)} w,
\qquad H^{(i)}=\operatorname{Hess}\rho_i(\alpha,\beta)\ \text{(classical)}.$$
Here $H^{(i)}$ is computed as the genuine second derivative of $t\mapsto\gamma_i(t)$
along the line $(\alpha+tu,\beta+tv)$, i.e. it is a **classical** Hessian.

BGLS (`thm:bgls`) only delivers that $\sigma_k(z)=\sum_{i\le k}\lambda_i(z)$ is a
**convex function**, hence its Hessian is PSD *in the distributional / a.e.*
sense. The proof then writes
$$\operatorname{Hess}L=\sum_{k<n}(c_k-c_{k+1})\operatorname{Hess}\sigma_k$$
and concludes $\sum_i\gamma_i H^{(i)}\succeq0$. This requires two unstated facts
at the point $(\alpha,\beta)$:

1. each $\sigma_k$ is **classically $C^2$** there (so "Hess $\sigma_k$" is a
   genuine matrix, not just a distribution), and
2. $\operatorname{Hess}\sigma_k=\sum_{i\le k}\operatorname{Hess}\lambda_i$ with
   each $\operatorname{Hess}\lambda_i$ the classical Hessian appearing in
   Lemma `lem:hess` (after the affine-in-$x$ reduction).

The solution's only justification is the remark *"Where the spectrum is
degenerate, convexity still gives Hess $\sigma_k$ PSD in the distributional
sense, which is all that is needed for the averaging."* **This is not valid:** a
distributional PSD Hessian cannot be substituted into the *pointwise* quadratic
form $\sum_i\gamma_i\,w^\ast H^{(i)} w$ of Lemma `lem:hess`. One must produce
the actual pointwise matrices and show they are PSD.

**Why it is nonetheless closable (suggested fix).** After the reduction to
simple roots, the roots $\gamma_j=\rho_j(\alpha,\beta)$ are pairwise distinct,
so the hyperbolic eigenvalues $\lambda_i(z)=x-\rho_{n+1-i}(a,b)$ of the Walsh
polynomial $f$ have pairwise-distinct values at $z_0=(0,\alpha,\beta)$ (a
spectral gap $\lambda_k>\lambda_{k+1}$ for all $k$). By the **analytic implicit
function theorem** applied to the simple linear factor of $t\mapsto f(z-te_1)$,
each $\lambda_i$ (equivalently each $\rho_j$) is real-analytic in a neighborhood
of $z_0$; hence each $\sigma_k$ is $C^2$ there and
$\operatorname{Hess}\sigma_k=\sum_{i\le k}\operatorname{Hess}\lambda_i\succeq0$
by BGLS convexity. This makes the substitution into Lemma `lem:hess`
legitimate. The fix is to insert this lemma and to **delete** the distributional
remark (which, as written, asserts an invalid substitution).

**Numerical confirmation that the conclusion is true (so this is a rigor gap,
not a falsity):** the operator $\sum_i\gamma_i H^{(i)}$ restricted to the
mean-zero subspace $V$ has minimum eigenvalue $\approx 0$ (PSD, with kernel) in
all random trials $2\le n\le4$; equivalently each "sum of the $k$ smallest roots
of $p\boxplus_n q$" is concave in $(a,b)$ on $V$.
